query: What are the requirements and fees required to submit a valid interconnection application for a large generator in ISO New England, based on the latest ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide and ISO-NE Tariff Schedule 22 Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP)?

steps.reasoning: We need answer: requirements and fees to submit valid interconnection application for large generator in ISO New England, based on latest ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide and ISO-NE Tariff Schedule 22 LGIP. Need current accurate info. We must search web. Need include application requirements, forms, studies, deposits/fees. Cite sources. Possibly ISO-NE website and tariff. We can use web search. Let's do searches.

tool_input.query: ISO New England Interconnection Process Guide large generator interconnection application requirements fees Schedule 22 LGIP

steps.reasoning: Let me now get the actual Schedule 22 LGIP PDF and the Appendix 1 to read the specific requirements for valid IR and fees. The search results gave me snippets but I need full details.

tool_input.command:
python -c "
import requests, re, os, json, textwrap
url='https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/regulatory/tariff/sect_2/sch22/sch_22_lgip.pdf'
open('/work/sch22.pdf','wb').write(requests.get(url).content)
print('downloaded', os.path.getsize('/work/sch22.pdf'))
"
grep -n -i "commercial readiness" /work/sch22.txt | head -30 && echo "---" && grep -n -i "withdraw" /work/sch22.txt | head -20

sed -n '1230,1260p' /work/sch22.txt && echo "---Appendix 1" && python -c "
import requests, os
url='https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf'
open('/work/app1.pdf','wb').write(requests.get(url).content)
print('app1 size', os.path.getsize('/work/app1.pdf'))
" && pdftotext /work/app1.pdf /work/app1.txt && sed -n '1,120p' /work/app1.txt

      "text": "## ISO-NE Responses to New Process Related Questions Raised in the Context of Order No. 2023 Compliance Discussions\n\nJanuary 18, 2024 General Notes Applicable to the ISO Responses Note there is overlap between many of the questions raised on the context of Order No. 2023 received by the ISO. The responses in this document reflect the ISO’s views of and expected compliance with Order No. 2023 at the time of its publication. These ISO views and proposals may change before the ISO presents its final Order No. 2023 compliance proposal. Participants should continually check for updates, follow the applicable stakeholder committee discussions and review the final version of the rules. Questions and Responses Q1: Will there be two different heat maps: one for NRIS and one for CNRIS? Will ISO-NE require that customers obtain CEII access to view the heat map(s)? Will Transmission Owners have a role in the creating and maintaining the heat map(s)? Response: Consistent with the requirements of Order No. 2023, which requires the heatmap to approximate capacity interconnection service and not energy interconnection service, ISO-NE will propose in the compliance filing that its heatmap will approximate CNR Interconnection Service. CEII access will not be required. ISO-NE is responsible for the creation and maintenance of the heat map. Transmission Owners will be responsible for providing ISO-NE with any supporting data, as needed. Q2: Will ISO-NE comply with Order 2023 by only allowing IR submittal during the Cluster Request Window? Response: Yes, ISO-NE will only allow Interconnection Request submittal during the Cluster Request Window, see proposed Section 3.4.2 of the LGIP. Q3: When is the technical data for the cluster study due? Will there be time for ISO-NE to review the data prior to the cluster study start?\n\nResponse: All data and models included in Appendix 1 of the IR and its associated attachments must be \nprovided with the Interconnection Request during the Cluster Request Window (CRW), see Section 3.4.2 \nof the LGIP. In order for ISO-NE to properly review and give the customer the full time to cure any \ndeficiencies, it is recommended to submit IR and data at least 15 business days prior to close of CRW. \nISO-NE will have five (5) Business Days to review the data and the Interconnection Customer will have \nup to ten (10) Business Days to cure any data deficiencies, but no later than the close of the Cluster \nRequest Window (Section 3.4.4 of LGIP). \nQ4: Will Cluster Request Windows open the same time every year, or will they depend on the stage that \nthe previous cluster cycle is in? Will Cluster Request Windows be delayed by additional restudies? Will \ncustomers have access to an updated heat map, cluster (re)study results, and base case from the most \nrecent cluster prior to entering the next cluster study? Will ISO-NE allow customers to update their \ntechnical data prior to the cluster study start if the cluster study start is delayed by a previous cluster \nneeding a restudy? \nResponse: The Initial Cluster Request Window will open sixty (60) Calendar Days after the conclusion of \nthe Transitional Study process. Subsequent Cluster Request Windows will open thirty (30) Calendar Days \nafter ISO-NE issues Facilities Study Agreements and/or draft Interconnection Agreements (if the \nFacilities Study is waived) to all Interconnection Customers in the previous Cluster Study. This allows \nfor the new cluster to have access to updated heat map, Cluster Study and Cluster Restudy results (if \napplicable), and the most recent Base Case. Will ISO-NE’s technology specific acreage \nrequirements allow for flexibility where specific proposals may vary from typical acreage requirements? \nResponse: ISO-NE will post acreage requirements for Site Control for Generating Facilities on its website. \nISO-NE will allow for flexibility where specific proposals may vary from typical acreage requirements, \nprovided the Interconnection Customer explains the variance. \nQ7: Are customers required to submit the signed site control between themselves and the landowner or \nmust it be publically recorded at the local registry of deeds before submission? \nResponse: ISO-NE will accept signed documentation establishing that the customer (not an Affiliate or \nParent Company) has Site Control. The definition of Site Control can be found in Section I of the LGIP. \n\nQ8: Will ISO-NE require proof of site control for a generator lead line? Will ISO-NE require 100% site \ncontrol of interconnection customer interconnection facilities, including gen lead tie lines by the time \nthe IA executed? \nResponse: Site Control for the generator lead line is not required with the Interconnection Request. \nSiting approval for Interconnection Facilities is a requirement under the LGIA Appendix B Milestones, on \na date agreed to by IC & ITO. 100% Site Control for the Generating Facility is required under Section \n3.4.2 (iv) of the LGIP. \nQ9: If an Interconnection Request includes an HVDC interconnection, will ISO-NE require the same site \ncontrol for the generator’s HVDC converter that would be required if the HVDC line had been proposed \nseparately as an ETU? \nResponse: ISO-NE will not require site control for the generator’s HVDC converter. \nQ10: Will ISO-NE require Transmission Owners and Affected Parties to attend the cluster-wide Scoping \nMeeting? Will there be a penalty if Transmission Owners do not attend the cluster-wide Scoping \nMeeting? Will Affected Parties (such as various municipal departments) maintain their current level of \ninvolvement in studies? \nResponse: Yes, ISO-NE will require Transmission Owners to attend the cluster-wide Scoping Meeting and \nwill invite Internal Affected Parties (to the extent they are able to be identified at this stage of the \nprocess), which will be defined under Section 1 of the LGIP. Internal Affected Parties will maintain their \ncurrent level of involvement in studies. No penalties will be imposed on Transmission Owners who do \nnot attend. \nQ11: How will project specific CEII information be protected during the cluster-wide Scoping Meeting? \nHow does ISO-NE plan to ensure all customers have the opportunity to have their questions answered \nduring the cluster-wide Scoping Meeting? Will there be a system for customers to submit their \nquestions where ISO-NE would then post written answers for all customers in the cluster to see? \nResponse: Project specific CEII will be protected by ensuring all parties attending the Scoping Meeting 1) \nhave CEII clearance, and 2) have signed a non-disclosure agreement (NDA) prior to the group Cluster \nStudy Scoping Meeting. The NDA will provide confidentiality of commercially sensitive information \npertaining to other Interconnection Customers. ISO-NE will determine the format of Scoping Meetings \nprior to the opening of the first Cluster Request Window. \nQ12: Will ISO-NE allow an alternative POI that is electrically proximate to be discussed during the \ncustomer engagement window and included in the cluster study agreement?       "text": "## ISO-NE Responses to New Process Related Questions Raised in the Context of Order No. 2023 Compliance Discussions\n\nJanuary 18, 2024 General Notes Applicable to the ISO Responses Note there is overlap between many of the questions raised on the context of Order No. 2023 received by the ISO. Not all questions received by the ISO are documented below, but the questions and answers documented below attempt to provide responses to all received questions. The responses in this document reflect the ISO’s views of and expected compliance with Order No. 2023 at the time of its publication. These ISO views and proposals may change before the ISO presents its final Order No. 2023 compliance proposal. Participants should continually check for updates, follow the applicable stakeholder committee discussions and review the final version of the rules. Questions and Responses Q1: Will there be two different heat maps: one for NRIS and one for CNRIS? Will ISO-NE require that customers obtain CEII access to view the heat map(s)? Will Transmission Owners have a role in the creating and maintaining the heat map(s)? Response: Consistent with the requirements of Order No. 2023, which requires the heatmap to approximate capacity interconnection service and not energy interconnection service, ISO-NE will propose in the compliance filing that its heatmap will approximate CNR Interconnection Service. CEII access will not be required. ISO-NE is responsible for the creation and maintenance of the heat map. Transmission Owners will be responsible for providing ISO-NE with any supporting data, as needed. Q2: Will ISO-NE comply with Order 2023 by only allowing IR submittal during the Cluster Request Window? Response: Yes, ISO-NE will only allow Interconnection Request submittal during the Cluster Request Window, see proposed Section 3.4.2 of the LGIP. Q3: When is the technical data for the cluster study due? Will there be time for ISO-NE to review the data prior to the cluster study start?\n\nResponse: All data and models included in Appendix 1 of the IR and its associated attachments must be \nprovided with the Interconnection Request during the Cluster Request Window (CRW), see Section 3.4.2 \nof the LGIP. In order for ISO-NE to properly review and give the customer the full time to cure any \ndeficiencies, it is recommended to submit IR and data at least 15 business days prior to close of CRW. \nISO-NE will have five (5) Business Days to review the data and the Interconnection Customer will have \nup to ten (10) Business Days to cure any data deficiencies, but no later than the close of the Cluster \nRequest Window (Section 3.4.4 of LGIP). \nQ4: Will Cluster Request Windows open the same time every year, or will they depend on the stage that \nthe previous cluster cycle is in? Will Cluster Request Windows be delayed by additional restudies? Will \ncustomers have access to an updated heat map, cluster (re)study results, and base case from the most \nrecent cluster prior to entering the next cluster study? Will ISO-NE allow customers to update their \ntechnical data prior to the cluster study start if the cluster study start is delayed by a previous cluster \nneeding a restudy? \nResponse: The Initial Cluster Request Window will open sixty (60) Calendar Days after the conclusion of \nthe Transitional Study process. Subsequent Cluster Request Windows will open thirty (30) Calendar Days \nafter ISO-NE issues Facilities Study Agreements and/or draft Interconnection Agreements (if the \nFacilities Study is waived) to all Interconnection Customers in the previous Cluster Study. ISO-NE will \nprovide advance notice on its public website when each Cluster Request Window will open. This allows \nfor the new cluster to have access to updated heat map, Cluster Study and Cluster Restudy results (if \napplicable), and the most recent Base Case. Customers will be required to submit all required data and \nmodels at the time of their Interconnection Request submission. \nQ5: Can customers submit a separate IR for NR Interconnection Service and CNR Interconnection \nService for the same project in the same cluster? \nResponse: No, at the time of submission customers must request either CNR Interconnection Service or \nNR Interconnection Service under Section 3.2 of the LGIP. \nQ6: Does Order 2023 require the ISO-NE to maintain acreage requirements for infrastructure (i.e. \nsubstations, line breaks by voltage or breaker type)? Will ISO-NE’s technology specific acreage \nrequirements allow for flexibility where specific proposals may vary from typical acreage requirements? \nResponse: ISO-NE will post acreage requirements for Site Control for Generating Facilities on its website. \nISO-NE will allow for flexibility where specific proposals may vary from typical acreage requirements, \nprovided the Interconnection Customer explains the variance. \nQ7: Are customers required to submit the signed site control between themselves and the landowner or \nmust it be publically recorded at the local registry of deeds before submission? \nResponse: ISO-NE will accept signed documentation establishing that the customer (not an Affiliate or \nParent Company) has Site Control. The definition of Site Control can be found in Section I of the LGIP. \n\nQ8: Will ISO-NE require proof of site control for a generator lead line? Will ISO-NE require 100% site \ncontrol of interconnection customer interconnection facilities, including gen lead tie lines by the time \nthe IA executed? \nResponse: Site Control for the generator lead line is not required with the Interconnection Request. \nSiting approval for Interconnection Facilities is a requirement under the LGIA Appendix B Milestones, on \na date agreed to by IC & ITO. 100% Site Control for the Generating Facility is required under Section \n3.4.2 (iv) of the LGIP. \nQ9: If an Interconnection Request includes an HVDC interconnection, will ISO-NE require the same site \ncontrol for the generator’s HVDC converter that would be required if the HVDC line had been proposed \nseparately as an ETU? \nResponse: ISO-NE will not require site control for the generator’s HVDC converter. \nQ10: Will ISO-NE require Transmission Owners and Affected Parties to attend the cluster-wide Scoping \nMeeting? Will there be a penalty if Transmission Owners do not attend the cluster-wide Scoping \nMeeting? Will Affected Parties (such as various municipal departments) maintain their current level of \ninvolvement in studies? \nResponse: Yes, ISO-NE will require Transmission Owners to attend the cluster-wide Scoping Meeting and \nwill invite Internal Affected Parties (to the extent they are able to be identified at this stage of the \nprocess), which will be defined under Section 1 of the LGIP. Internal Affected Parties will maintain their \ncurrent level of involvement in studies. No penalties will be imposed on Transmission Owners who do \nnot attend. \nQ11: How will project specific CEII information be protected during the cluster-wide Scoping Meeting? \nHow does ISO-NE plan to ensure all customers have the opportunity to have their questions answered \nduring the cluster-wide Scoping Meeting? Will there be a system for customers to submit their \nquestions where ISO-NE would then post written answers for all customers in the cluster to see? \nResponse: Project specific CEII will be protected by ensuring all parties attending the Scoping Meeting 1) \nhave CEII clearance, and 2) have signed a non-disclosure agreement (NDA) prior to the group Cluster \nStudy Scoping Meeting. The NDA will provide confidentiality of commercially sensitive information \npertaining to other Interconnection Customers. ISO-NE will determine the format of Scoping Meetings \nprior to the opening of the first Cluster Request Window. \nQ12: Will ISO-NE allow an alternative POI that is electrically proximate to be discussed during the \ncustomer engagement window and included in the cluster study agreement?       "text": "SEPTEMBER 27, 2023 | NEPOOL TRANSMISSION COMMITTEE | DOUBLETREE HOTEL, WESTBOROUGH, MA\n\nAl McBride D I R E C T O R | T R A N S M I S S I O N S E R V I C E S & R E S O U R C E Q U A L I F I C A T I O N\n\nProposed Compliance Overview\n\n## Order 2023 - Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\n##### Project Title: Order No. 2023 Compliance Proposed Effective Date: March 2024\n\n• ISO New England is preparing its compliance with Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC”) Order No. 2023 \n• This presentation provides: – A summary description of the Order – An overview of ISO’s proposed compliance – Anticipated stakeholder schedule \n\n### SUMMARY DESCRIPTION OF ORDER 2023\n\n### Order No. 2023 Development\n\n• On July 28, 2023, FERC issued Order No. 2023, an approximately 1500-page unanimous Final Rule adopting significant reforms to the standard Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (“LGIP”) and Small Generator Interconnection Procedure (“SGIP”) \n• The reforms build on the standardized procedures that FERC established in Order Nos. 2003, 2006 and 845 to address interconnection queue backlogs, improve certainty, and prevent undue discrimination for new technologies \n• Order No. 2023 adopts most of the reforms proposed in the July 5, 2022 Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (“NOPR”) \n\n### The Need for Reform\n\n• In Order No. 2023, FERC affirmed their preliminary findings that the existing standardized interconnection procedures and agreements are insufficient to ensure interconnection to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner – The growth of new resources seeking to interconnect and the differing characteristics of those resources have created new challenges for the process \n• These new challenges contribute to queue backlogs and uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting, increasing costs for consumers, which, in turn, can create reliability issues, as needed new generating facilities are unable to come online in an efficient and timely manner \n\n### The Need for Reform, continued\n\n• FERC concludes that, absent reforms, the current process will continue to cause queue backlogs, longer development timelines, and increased uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting to the transmission system – Accordingly, pursuant to Federal Power Act Section 206, FERC adopted significant, non-regionally tailored, reforms in the standard LGIP and SGIP \n\n### Order No. 2023 Compliance\n\n• FERC will evaluate compliance filings in light of the independent entity variations standard – Permits ISO-NE to seek to preserve certain variations implemented over the years to improve queue management – However, the order requires significant changes to the manner in which ISO-NE administers the interconnection processes, as well as how interconnection customers are expected to participate in those processes \n• Order No. 2023 was published in the Federal Register on September 6, 2023, setting a compliance deadline of December 5, 2023 \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order Transition to First-Ready First-Served Cluster Process\n\n• Requires that transmission providers make several changes to transition to a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, rather than the current serial first-come, first-served study process – Cluster study process – Increased financial commitments for interconnection customers to enter and remain in the process – New mechanisms for interconnection customers to
